coredata.dc:description: Due to the overuse of antimicrobial drugs, bacterial resistance became an urgent problem to be solved. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high photodynamic antibacterial activity were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method and introduced into bacterial cellulose (BC) dispersion solution. Through a wet-spinning and wet-twisting processing strategy, bionic ordering nanocomposite macrofiber (BC/CQDs-based yarn) based on BC were obtained. The results showed that BC/CQDs-based yarn had excellent tensile strength (226.8 MPa) and elongation (22.2 %). Utilizing the light-driven generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), BC/CQDs-based yarn demonstrated remarkable antibacterial efficacy, with 99.9999 % (6 log, P < 0.0001) and 96.54 % (1.46 log, P < 0.0004) effectiveness against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. At the same time, BC/CQDs-based yarn also displayed the characteristics of photothermal, fluorescent, and biodegradability. In summary, the application potential of BC/CQDs-based yarn is significant, opening up a new strategy for the development of sustainable green weaving and bio-based multi-function yarn.

(d) UV–visible absorption spectra of the CQDs obtained at various concentrations. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs generated at different excitation wavelengths. (f) The time change curve of CQDs degradation ABDA solution under light condition was recorded every 5 min. (g) Raman spectrum of CQD.

Preparation and microstructure of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. (a) Flow chart of the preparation process of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. (b) Physical image of the ternary co-spinning solution. (c) Viscosity-shear rate curves of spinning solutions containing different substances. (d–e) Physical images of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. (f–i) SEM images of cross-section, longitudinal section, knotted BC/CQDs-based macrofiber, and small-angle X-ray scattering of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. (j) Fiber twisting diagram.

Mechanical properties and structural characterization of the biomimetic BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and yarn with a hierarchically spiral ordered structure. (a) Stress-strain curves of the Alg, BC/Alg, and BC/Alg/CQDs macrofibers obtained, along with the corresponding (b) spider web diagram depicting different samples' mechanical parameters. (c) A photograph showing the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber effortlessly lifting a 500 g weight. Scale bar, 500 μm. (j) The tensile strength and strain of yarn with different twist.

(b) Schematic diagram illustrating the photothermal and photodynamic antibacterial mechanism of the prepared BC/CQDs-based yarn. (c) Antibacterial effects of different samples on E. coli under both light-driven and dark room conditions. (d–e) SEM images depicting the antibacterial effect of BC/CQDs-based yarn against E. coli before and after exposure to visible light. (f) Antibacterial effects of different samples on S. aureus under both light and dark room conditions. Fluorescence and biodegradable properties of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and yarn. (a) Fabrics with fluorescence and antibacterial properties were created using a weaving process. (b) BC/CQDs-based yarn was woven into textiles with various patterns. (c) Photograph depicting the fluorescent rendering of yarn woven into different letters under a UV lamp. (f) SEM image of macrofiber degradation for 36 h. Recorded every 12 h.

head.ce:title: Robust integration of light-driven carbon quantum dots with bacterial cellulose enables excellent mechanical and antibacterial biodegradable yarn

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the overuse of antimicrobial drugs, bacterial resistance became an urgent problem to be solved. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high photodynamic antibacterial activity were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method and introduced into bacterial cellulose (BC) dispersion solution. Through a wet-spinning and wet-twisting processing strategy, bionic ordering nanocomposite macrofiber (BC/CQDs-based yarn) based on BC were obtained. The results showed that BC/CQDs-based yarn had excellent tensile strength (226.8 MPa) and elongation (22.2 %). Utilizing the light-driven generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), BC/CQDs-based yarn demonstrated remarkable antibacterial efficacy, with 99.9999 % (6 log, P < 0.0001) and 96.54 % (1.46 log, P < 0.0004) effectiveness against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. At the same time, BC/CQDs-based yarn also displayed the characteristics of photothermal, fluorescent, and biodegradability. In summary, the application potential of BC/CQDs-based yarn is significant, opening up a new strategy for the development of sustainable green weaving and bio-based multi-function yarn.

Schematic diagram of preparation of macrofiber and schematic diagram of weaving, fluorescence and antibacterial effect.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: In the context of the global COVID-19 epidemic, researchers have been increasingly concerned about the detrimental impact of pathogenic microorganisms on human health [1]. Antibiotics, hailed as one of the greatest inventions of the 20th century, have encountered a growing challenge as bacteria develop resistance due to drug abuse and their enhanced ability to adapt to the environment [2,3]. Treating antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections is a formidable task that often leads to complications and even fatalities [4]. Chemical drugs and antimicrobials remain the primary clinical approach for combating bacterial and viral diseases, playing a vital role in the advancement of human society's well-being [5,6]. However, the currently available antibacterial agents have limitations. For instance, common metal ion antibacterial agents, despite their excellent antibacterial properties, suffer from complex preparation processes and susceptibility to discoloration [7]. Moreover, long-term use of metal ion antimicrobials may result in a gradual decline in antimicrobial effectiveness and potential environmental consequences. Quaternary ammonium salt antibacterial agents exhibit high antibacterial efficiency but are prone to solvent precipitation and decomposition into toxic byproducts [8]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop and design novel antibacterial agents that are cost-effective, possess high antibacterial activity, offer a wide range of effectiveness, and are environmentally friendly.

In this context, the photodynamic inactivation (PDI) mechanism based on reactive oxygen species (ROS) has been proposed as a novel strategy to eliminate bacteria with broad-spectrum antibacterial effects. Among them, rhodamine b, methyl orange, methylene blue, hypocrellin and CQDs are widely used photosensitizers at present [9]. It is noteworthy that CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials possessing unique properties, including easy synthesis, good biocompatibility, excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions, and controllable optical characteristics [10–12]. These properties render them highly promising for applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, anti-inflammatory and antioxidant, wastewater treatment [13–17]. The main synthesis methods for CQDs encompass acid oxidation, pyrolysis, hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and others [18–21]. The surface of CQD contains abundant groups and can produce 1O2,·OH, superoxide radical (O2−) and so on [22], and under the condition of light, which can produce oxidative stress reaction with bacteria and cause oxidative damage to biological macromolecules such as proteins and DAN of bacteria, thus leading to cell wall damage and bacterial death (not all CQDs can produce ROS) [23]. The use of CQD as a photosensitized agent for antibacterial has been studied. For example, Markovic et al. [24], developed an antibacterial polyurethane composite film encapsulated with hydrophobic riboflavin-based carbon polymer points, which produced 1O2 and O2− under the action of low power blue light, which could effectively remove bacteria. Ristic et al. [25], used electrochemical method to prepare GQD that can produce ROS when photoexcited, which can cause oxidative stress damage to bacteria and increase the permeability of cell membrane. CQD are water-soluble substances and need to be loaded on a carrier to better play their role. For instance, Zmejkoski et al. [26], GQD was loaded onto BC hydrogel by impregnating method, which as a wound dressing has good biocompatibility, antibacterial and promoting wound healing. Nie et al. [27], prepared CQD by hydrothermal method, and then prepared PAN-CQDs membrane which can produce 1O2 efficiently by electrospinning process, and its inactivation rate of E. coli could reach 99.9999+%. Wang et al. [28], successfully prepared a BC/CQDs@PCN-224 membrane using an in-situ biosynthesis strategy, which exhibited excellent antibacterial activity. In the “post-epidemic era”, researchers are paying more attention to textiles with antibacterial functions, so it is of great significance to combine CQDs with textile processing technology.

In recent years, the depletion of non-renewable resources such as petroleum has made the development of green and renewable resources urgent [29]. In the realm of chemical fibers, commonly used materials like polyester, acrylic, and nylon are derived from petroleum [30–32], resulting in resource waste and adverse effects such as static electricity generation and human allergies during usage [33]. Therefore, the development of new, environmentally friendly, and renewable natural biomass fibers is imperative. Among these alternatives, BC has garnered significant attention due to its sustainability and environmental friendliness [34–37]. Leveraging the exceptional physical and chemical properties of BC, our research group has conducted a series of studies in various fields, including water treatment, sensors, biomedicine, and energy storage, through in-situ assembly and surface functionalization [35,38–40]. In the context of a dual‑carbon background, in order to obtain excellent mechanical properties, researchers have shown a preference for biomass-derived macrofibers prepared through wet-spinning or wet-twisting methods using BC. For instance, Wang et al. [41], successfully produced high-performance ultrafine yarns by wet-spinning and wet-twisting ultra-long BC nanofibers. Liang et al. [42], developed high-strength, super-stretchable helical BC fibers by constructing a “self-fiber-reinforced structure”. Zhao et al. [43], reported continuous high-performance BC coarse fibers by implementing graded stretching during spinning. More recently, Guan et al. [44], drew inspiration from lotus flowers and fabricated hydrogel BC fibers with exceptional strength, toughness, and stretchability by simulating the spiral structure. Building upon these previous findings, thanks to the rich functional groups and large specific surface area of BC itself, physically doped or functionally modified BC fibers will further expand the application of functional textiles.

Herein, this paper studied the preparation of biomass BC based functional yarn. The process involved using BC as the macrofiber base, CQDs as functional material and sodium alginate (Alg) as soft mechanism, and the specific preparation process included wet-spinning and wet-twisting process. The physical and chemical properties of CQDs, macrofiber and yarn were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectrum, scanning electron fiberscope (SEM), rotational rheological analysis, two-dimensional multifunction small-angle X-ray scattering, strength analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), UV/visible spectrophotometer (UV), fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL), confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and infrared thermal imaging methods. Finally, the light-driven antibacterial and biodegradable properties of the yarns were tested by plate counting method and enzymatic hydrolysis method.

Mannitol (≥98.0 %), tryptone (≥BR), yeast powder, sodium hydroxide (≥AR), soy tryptone, sodium chloride (≥99.5 %), cellulase (≥BR), agar (≥BR), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5 %), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4, ≥99.0 %), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, ≥99.5 %), and Tween 80 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium alginate (Alg), calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥96.0 %), 1.5-diamine naphthalene (1,5-DAN, >97.0 %), citric acid (CA, ≥99.0 %), ethanol (≥95.0 %), 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis (methylene) dimalonic acid (ABDA, 90 %) and methyl violet (MV) were purchased from m Shanghai Vita Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were analytical grade reagents and could be used without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method [45–47]. The synthesis process involved the following steps: CA (260 mg, 0.68 mM) and 1,5-DAN (520 mg, 1.64 mM) were added to anhydrous ethanol (260 mL). After 20 min of ultrasonic dispersion, the mixture was transferred to a reaction kettle lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at 200 °C in a blast oven for 9 h. Following natural cooling to room temperature, the solution was separated from ethanol using rotary evaporation (80 °C). The resulting solution was then dissolved in deionized water. Impurities were removed through filtration and dialysis. The resulting brown CQDs powder was freeze-dried and stored in a dark refrigerator.

The BC membrane was prepared in our laboratory [48], and the specific steps were as follows. The BC culture medium was prepared by combining 25 g/L mannitol, 5 g/L yeast powder, and 3 g/L tryptone. The culture medium was divided into 250 mL conical bottles and sterilized at high temperature and pressure (121 °C, 0.103 MPa). Bacteria were then inoculated onto a super-clean table. The culture medium was inoculated with 10 % (v/v) acetobacter xylem solution, homogenized in the conical bottles, sealed with a film, and placed in a constant temperature incubator at 30 °C for 7 d. The BC was carefully extracted using tweezers and transferred to a beaker. After deionizing cleaning, it was boiled in a constant temperature water bath at 80 °C with a 4 wt% NaOH solution for 16 h. Subsequently, it was rinsed with deionized water multiple times until the pH became neutral, effectively removing any residual culture and bacterial solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and BC/CQDs-based yarn
ce:section.ce:para: Pure BC was added to deionized water and homogenized for 60 min using a high-speed disperser. Subsequently, it was sonicated for 30 min in a cell disruptor to obtain BC suspensions with different concentrations (10 mg/mL, 15 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 25 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL). Alg at varying concentrations was added to the BC suspensions (10 mg/mL, 15 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 25 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL), followed by the addition of CQDs (1 mg/mL). The mixture was stirred for 30 min using a constant temperature magnetic stirrer, resulting in a uniform ternary co-spinning solution. The spinning solution was extruded into a coagulation bath containing CaCl2 (1.1 g/100 mL) through a syringe, with different spinning speeds set (1 mL/min, 2 mL/min, 3 mL/min, 4 mL/min, 5 mL/min). After spinning and shaping, the fibers were air-dried at room temperature to obtain BC/CQDs-based macrofibers. To prepare BC/CQDs-based yarn, single BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were twisted using a wet twisting process to obtain 2-ply and 4-ply yarns.

The size of the synthesized CQDs nanoparticles was observed and analyzed using TEM, while SEM was utilized to observe the surface structure and microstructure of the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber, BC/CQDs-based yarn, E. coli, and S. aureus. The composition and structure of CQD were explored by XPS and Raman. The rheological properties of the spinning solution were tested using a rotational rheometer, and the macrofiber orientation was characterized using a two-dimensional multifunctional small-angle X-ray scattering instrument. The thermodynamic properties of the prepared samples were characterized using a TGA and a universal tensile testing machine. The chemical structure and optical properties of CQDs and macrofiber were analyzed using a FT-IR, UV, and PL. The photoluminescence behavior of the sample was investigated by CLSM.

ABDA solution was used to detect whether the CQDs could produce 1O2 under light conditions. The specific step involved adding 3 mg of CQDs to a 3 mL ABDA solution with a concentration of 10 μM. The mixture was then irradiated under a xenon lamp (390 nm–780 nm range of light) for 40 min, and the fluorescence intensity of ABDA solution was measured every 5 min. Pure ABDA solution was used as the control group and was placed in both light and no light environments, respectively.
(2)
OH detection [40]

MV solution was used to detect whether the CQDs could produce ·OH under light conditions. The specific procedure involved adding 3 mg of CQDs to a 3 mL MV solution with a concentration of 10 μM. The mixture was then irradiated with a handheld Laser pointer (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2, HTPOW Laser Limited) for 60 min. The absorption spectrum of the solution and the absorbance at the characteristic wavelength of 681 nm were measured every 10 min. Pure MV solution was used as the control group and was placed in both light and no light environments, respectively.

First, the culture medium and plate of S. aureus and E. coli were prepared (the culture medium of S. aureus consisted of 15 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L soybean peptone and 5 g/L NaCl, and the plate consisted of adding 15 g/L agar on the basis of the culture medium; The culture medium of E. coli was 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract and 10 g/L NaCl, and the plate was prepared with 15 g/L agar added on the basis of the culture medium), which was sterilized at high temperature and cooled for use. Then 0.1 mL of the pro-bacteria solution was absorbed into a centrifuge tube containing 7 mL of culture solution using a pipette, wrapped in tin foil and placed in a constant temperature shaking table (120 r/min, 37 °C) for culture, and the appropriate concentration of microbial strains was 108–109 CFU/mL. Put the cultured bacterial solution into the centrifuge at a speed of 10,000 r/min for 10 min, pour the supernatant after completion, add the same volume of PBS buffer (170 mM NaCl, 3.4 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, 1.8 mM KH2PO4, 0.05 % Tween 80), and shake evenly for reserve use.

The same number of samples were put into the 24-well plate, and each group of samples was set up with 3 groups of parallel experiments and 3 groups of blank experiments, which were divided into dark group and light-driven group. 0.1 mL of the above bacterial solution was added to the surface of each sample, so that it was fully in contact with the sample for 30 min, in which the dark group was completely wrapped with tin foil, and the light-driven group was placed under 500 W xenon lam to receive light. After the end, 0.9 mL PBS buffer was added into the 24 well plates of the dark group and the light group, and the bacterial solution in each well was diluted 6 times according to the concentration of 1:10 to form 6 bacterial solutions with different concentration gradients. Then, 10 μL bacterial solution was dribbled in agar plate according to low to high concentration gradient, and the bacterial solution was uniformly poured down and inverted, and cultured in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C in a dark room for 12 h. After the incubation, the colonies of each gradient were counted. The antibacterial rate of the BC/CQDs-based yarn was calculated as:
(1)
Antibacterial rate % = 1 −
N i
N 0
× 100 %
where N
0 is the maximum number of colonies in the count column of the original bacterial solution (no sample group), and N
i is the colony value under the gradient corresponding to the original bacterial solution after the sample is antibacterial.

Pure CQDs were placed on a slide, BC/CQDs-based yarn were sewn into a black substrate, and the photothermal properties of CQDs and yarn samples were tested under NIR light (100 W, 760 nm ≤ λ ≤ 5000 nm, sample distance: 20 cm). The change of surface temperature was recorded by infrared thermal imager, and the data was collected every 1 s.

Firstly, 100 mL PBS buffer was prepared, and appropriate amount of BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were wound and placed in the solution, and 0.5 g cellulase was added. Then the degradation experiment was carried out in a water bath at 50 °C. During the degradation process, photos were taken every 12 h to compare the degradation patterns of the macrofibers.

Initially, CQDs with fluorescence features and the ability to generate 1O2 and ·OH under light-driven conditions were prepared. The detailed fabrication process is as follows [27]: CA and 1,5-DAN were introduced into the ethanol solution to obtain a suspension containing CQDs through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. After impurities were removed by dialysis, the CQDs solution exhibited blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 1a). In order to study the prepared CQDs, the appearance morphology of CQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the synthesized CQDs showed a relatively uniform spherical morphology. At the same time, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of CQDs show that CQD has a graphene-like layered structure with a spacing of 0.31 nm. Further, the particle size analysis by Nano Measurer showed that the particle size distribution of CQDs were relatively uniform and presented a regular circle, as shown in Fig. 1c, with an average particle size of 5.18 ± 0.72 nm and a normal distribution. XPS was used to study the elemental state of CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1c, three main peaks appeared in the XPS spectrum, which were assigned to 530.1 eV (O1s), 398.4 eV (N1s) and 285.0 eV (C1s), accounting for 76.74 %, 9.35 % and 13.91 %, respectively. To investigate the optical properties of the CQDs solution, UV–visible absorption spectra of CQDs at different concentrations and fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths were recorded. Fig. 1d showed that CQDs presented the highest absorbance value at 330 nm, and the absorbance value increased with the increase of CQDs concentration. The fluorescence spectra showed that CQDs exhibited the strongest emission intensity at 420 nm (Fig. 1e). At the same time, CQDs had excellent photothermal conversion performance. As shown in Fig. S1a–b, when CQDs were placed on a slide, the temperature of CQDs rose rapidly when it was irradiated by NIR light. After 10 s, the temperature of CQDs reached 65.1 °C, and after 23.5 s, the temperature of CQDs reached 72.2 °C. When the NIR light irradiation was removed, the temperature of CQDs dropped rapidly, and the temperature was 34.8 °C after 15 s. In order to prove ROS generated by CQDs, the generation of 1O2 was first detected by ABDA method [51], as shown in Fig. 1f & Fig. S1d. With the increase of illumination time, due to the generation of 1O2, the fluorescence attenuation rate of ABDA gradually increased, reaching 89.67 % at 40 min. The fluorescence attenuation rate of ABDA was calculated by the following formula:
(2)
Flucrescence decay
%
=
F control
−
F treatment
F control
× 100

In the undoped CQDs and unilluminated control, the fluorescence attenuation rate of ABDA did not change much, indicating that CQDs and light were required in order to generate 1O2. Then, the production of ·OH was detected by MV [40], as shown in Fig. S1 (e–f). With the increase of illumination time, the absorbance at the characteristic wavelength of MV at 584 nm showed a linear decline, and the solution was gradually oxidized from purple to light purple. This showed that CQDs produced oxidizing ·OH under the condition of light. ROS is a highly active oxygen free radical capable of interacting with biological macromolecules such as DNA and proteins in bacteria [52], thus demonstrating excellent antibacterial properties. In order to further study the structure of CDQ, Raman spectroscopy was performed for CQD. As shown in Fig. 1g, the Raman peaks of CQD under 532 nm laser excitation were located at 1335 cm−1 (D) and 1580 cm−1 (G), corresponding to sp3 and sp2 carbon structures respectively. Among them, ID/IG = 0.95, which indicated that sp2 structure was dominant, and the prepared CQD was mainly graphite structure [53]. In summary, the above experimental results demonstrated that the resulting CQDs were effective photosensitizers with fluorescence properties and could produce ROS under light conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and its microstructure
ce:section.ce:para: The above photosensitizer CQDs and soft matrix Alg were immersed to BC suspension, and scalable BC/CQDs-based macrofiber with high orientation structure was prepared by simple wet-spinning and then wet-drafting strategy (Fig. 2a & Movie. S1). The detailed preparation process can be divided into the following three stages: In the first stage, the uniformly dispersed ternary spinning system (Fig. 2b) achieved ordered uniaxial orientation through the flow shear force under high-speed extrusion force [54]. It was forced to align parallel to the long axis of the needle due to the local shear force [55]. In the second stage, the spinning solution, flowing axially, entered a coagulation bath containing CaCl2. Through the rapid displacement of water molecules, coordination of metal ions, and hydrogen bonding between components, the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber with a biomimetic orientation structure was formed. In the third stage, the macrofiber underwent winding drafting using a winding roller, which further improved the molecular orientation of the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. Rheological properties are important parameters to prove the spinnability of the spinning solution. As observed in the Viscosity-shear rate curve (Fig. 2c & Fig. S2), as the shear rate increased, the spinning solution exhibited shear-thinning behavior, resulting in a decrease in viscosity and an increase in shear stress [56]. With an increase in solution concentration, the interaction between molecular chains strengthened, leading to an increase in the average friction coefficient of main chain atoms. In the Alg spinning solution, molecular chains oriented due to the shearing force, causing the disruption of physical cross-linking points formed by hydrogen bonds within and between molecules, consequently reducing solution viscosity [57]. The addition of BC and CQDs resulted in the formation of more intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds within the spinning solution, enhancing molecular forces and increasing viscosity. However, as shear stress increased, some of the entanglement was disrupted, leading to a decrease in viscosity. Through the optimization of the spinning process, BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were continuously prepared, as depicted in Fig. 2d–e. SEM and EDS Mapping images confirmed the tightly oriented structure, excellent mechanical flexibility and distribution of element of the BC-based macrofibers (Fig. 2f–i & Fig. S3). In order to improve the practicability of the resulting macrofiber, two BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were wet-twisted to reduce the gap between fibers and form a yarn with bionic orientation and hierarchical spiral structure, which proved the designability of macrofiber (Fig. 2j).

The above photosensitizer CQDs and soft matrix Alg were immersed to BC suspension, and scalable BC/CQDs-based macrofiber with high orientation structure was prepared by simple wet-spinning and then wet-drafting strategy (Fig. 2a & Movie. S1). The detailed preparation process can be divided into the following three stages: In the first stage, the uniformly dispersed ternary spinning system (Fig. 2b) achieved ordered uniaxial orientation through the flow shear force under high-speed extrusion force [54]. It was forced to align parallel to the long axis of the needle due to the local shear force [55]. In the second stage, the spinning solution, flowing axially, entered a coagulation bath containing CaCl2. Through the rapid displacement of water molecules, coordination of metal ions, and hydrogen bonding between components, the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber with a biomimetic orientation structure was formed. In the third stage, the macrofiber underwent winding drafting using a winding roller, which further improved the molecular orientation of the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. Rheological properties are important parameters to prove the spinnability of the spinning solution. As observed in the Viscosity-shear rate curve (Fig. 2c & Fig. S2), as the shear rate increased, the spinning solution exhibited shear-thinning behavior, resulting in a decrease in viscosity and an increase in shear stress [56]. With an increase in solution concentration, the interaction between molecular chains strengthened, leading to an increase in the average friction coefficient of main chain atoms. In the Alg spinning solution, molecular chains oriented due to the shearing force, causing the disruption of physical cross-linking points formed by hydrogen bonds within and between molecules, consequently reducing solution viscosity [57]. The addition of BC and CQDs resulted in the formation of more intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds within the spinning solution, enhancing molecular forces and increasing viscosity. However, as shear stress increased, some of the entanglement was disrupted, leading to a decrease in viscosity. Through the optimization of the spinning process, BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were continuously prepared, as depicted in Fig. 2d–e. SEM and EDS Mapping images confirmed the tightly oriented structure, excellent mechanical flexibility and distribution of element of the BC-based macrofibers (Fig. 2f–i & Fig. S3). In order to improve the practicability of the resulting macrofiber, two BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were wet-twisted to reduce the gap between fibers and form a yarn with bionic orientation and hierarchical spiral structure, which proved the designability of macrofiber (Fig. 2j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure and mechanical properties of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and yarn
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra confirmed the chemical structure of the designed samples. The peak at 3337 cm−1 was mainly attributed to the OH stretching vibration contained in each component of the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. The peak of the resulting macrofiber at 1417 cm−1 belonged to the absorption peak of the carboxyl group (COOH) in Alg molecules [58]. Additionally, the peak at 1641 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CC bond in the synthesized photosensitizer CQDs, consistent with previously reported literature (Fig. S4a) [59]. TGA data indicated that the prepared BC/CQDs-based macrofiber exhibited excellent thermal stability, and the introduction of Alg and CQDs contributed to its improved thermal stability, as demonstrated in Fig. S4b–c. Precisely, the mechanical strength and elongation of the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber obtained were crucial factors affecting its weaveability and practical applications. Fig. 3a–b displayed the mechanical properties of the various designed samples. The tensile breaking strength of the obtained Alg, BC/Alg, and BC/Alg/CQDs macrofibers (can also be called BC/CQDs-based macrofiber) were 151.8 MPa, 198.3 MPa, and 213.0 MPa, respectively, with corresponding elongations of 20.15 %, 16.30 %, and 17.05 % as shown in Fig. 3a–b and Fig. S5. It was worth noting that the BC/CQDs-based macrofiber prepared in this study exhibited superior mechanical strength compared to pure spinning Alg and BC/Alg macrofibers. With a diameter of 210 μm, it was capable of lifting a weight of 500 g (Fig. 3c). The excellent mechanical properties of the prepared BC/CQDs-based macrofiber can be attributed to the following factors (Fig. 3d): molecular orientation, intermolecular interaction, presence of functional groups, and cross-linked network structure. Interestingly, inspired by the spiral structure of plants, we construct biomimetic BC/CQDs-based yarn (helical-2 macrofibers) with hierarchically ordered structure, mechanically robust and high energy dissipation by simply wet-twisting with certain twist levels and wet-drafting two single BC/CQDs-based macrofibers, as shown in Fig. 3e. After that, the helical-4 macrofibers was fabricated with a certain twist according to the above similar method, as shown in Fig. 3f. The results showed that the mechanical strength and elongation of biomimetic helical-2 and helical-4 macrofibers after wet-twisting as compared to single BC/CQDs-based macrofiber were 218.1 MPa, 226.8 MPa and 19.8 %, 22.2 % (Fig. 3g), respectively. This enhancement in mechanical strength can be mainly attributed to the increased number of resultant macrofibers and the reduction of interfiber cavities or defects achieved through the bio-inspired helical structure design. Furthermore, the increase in elongation can be attributed to the gradual straightening of the macrofibers within each helical distribution in the yarn before breaking. This results in a significant improvement in tensile deformation before the fibers break, relative to the untwisted macrofiber. Obviously, the hierarchical helical macrofibers exhibited improved mechanical properties, with simultaneous increases in strength and elongation compared to the untwisted macrofiber. This engineering strategy provided a solution to the challenge of achieving a balance between mechanical strength and elongation in fibers. It is worth noting that the yarn twist angle is a crucial factor affecting the mechanical properties of the yarn (Fig. 3h). To further investigate the influence of the twisting degree on the mechanical properties of the biomimetic BC/CQDs-based yarn, we focused on the helical-2 macrofibers as an example. We prepared helical-2 macrofibers with twist angles of 5°, 10°, and 20°, as depicted in Fig. 3i. The results revealed that the mechanical strength of the yarn gradually increased with the increase in twist angle. However, the elongation initially increased and then decreased. This phenomenon can be mainly attributed to excessive twist, which leads to excessive adhesion between macrofibers, hindering the movement between macrofibers (Fig. 3j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties of light-driven BC/CQDs-based yarn
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a displayed a comparison of three different fibers wound around the yarn roller. Among these, A and B represented wet BC/Alg macrofiber and BC/CQDs-based macrofiber, respectively, both of which exhibited a particular level of transparency. Upon the addition of CQDs, the color of macrofiber transitioned to a light brown shade. Once dried, the macrofibers lost their transparency and emitted a blue fluorescence when subjected to an ultraviolet lamp. In contrast, C, a polyester spandex fiber, distinctly differed in appearance from A and B. The UV and PL properties of BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were shown in Fig. S6. It could be seen that after CQDs doping spinning, it still had UV characteristics and PL characteristics at 330 nm and 420 nm. The above results showed that the original properties of CQDs could still be maintained after doping CQDs into the macrofiber [60]. To further demonstrate the presence of CQDs in macrofibers, images of BC/CQDs-based macrofibers were observed by CLSM (Fig. S7). BC/CQDs-based macrofibers exhibited uniform fluorescence emission when excited with a 405 nm laser, indicating the existing of CQDs. The uniform fluorescence emission also provided evidence that CQDs were uniformly distributed in the fibers. Fig. 4b illustrates the photodynamic and photothermal antibacterial mechanism of the designed BC/CQDs-based yarn: Firstly, CQDs, acting as a photosensitization agent, absorbed energy and transitioned from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (Sn) when exposed to visible light. Afterward, as energy was released from the Sn state back to the S0 state, both thermal and fluorescence effects were generated. These effects exhibited excellent photothermal conversion performance and effectively inhibited the proliferation of pathogenic microorganisms [61]. At the same time, the electrons in this state could return to the excited triplet state (T1) through the intersystem transition (ISC), and then the T1 state would return to the S0 state through the photochemical process of the two mechanisms, wherein the I mechanism produced ·OH and the II mechanism produced 1O2, which could oxidize and destroy biological macromolecules such as proteins and DNA, thus achieving PDI of pathogenic microorganisms [40]. To further demonstrate the light-driven antibacterial activity of the BC/CQDs-based yarn, we investigated the bacteriostatic inactivation of E. coli in gram-negative bacteria and S. aureus in gram-positive bacteria in vitro. The antibacterial effects of pure BC, BC/Alg, and BC/CQDs-based yarn against E. coli and S. aureus were presented in Fig. 4c–h under visible light and dark room conditions. Light-driven conditions involved xenon lamp irradiation (500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, sample distance of 15 cm, and light duration of 30 min), while dark conditions were maintained using tinfoil-wrapped 24-well plates. As seen in Fig. 4c–h, pure BC and BC/Alg yarn exhibited almost no antibacterial effect on bacteria under both light and dark conditions. In contrast, the BC/CQDs-based yarn demonstrated remarkable inactivation efficiencies of 99.9999 % (6 log, P < 0.0001) against E. coli and 96.54 % (1.46 log, P < 0.0004) against S. aureus after 30 min of exposure to light. The inactivation effect of BC/CQDs-based yarn on E. coli was more obvious, which was possibly due to the limited diffusion distance of 1O2 in the water phase, and S. aureus tended to form grape-like clusters, affecting the entry of 1O2 into the bacteria [62]. Under dark conditions, BC/CQDs-based yarn also showed certain antibacterial properties, mainly because the surface of CQDs contains rich functional groups, such as amino groups, which can interact with the membrane of microbial cells, destroy the integrity of the cell membrane, and lead to cell death. SEM images revealed that the surfaces of the first two types of bacteria remained smooth and intact, with E. coli retaining its typical rod-like structure and S. aureus appearing spherical and aggregated into graphene clusters. After inactivation, E. coli and S. aureus showed a cracking state due to oxidative damage and photothermal synergism. BC/CQDs-based yarn also had a photothermal effect (PTT), as shown in Fig. 4i. Under the irradiation of a NIR lamp for 15 s, the temperature of BC/Alg yarn “J” increased from 25.2 °C to 48.4 °C due to heat conduction, and “N” and “U,” which contained CQDs, experienced a temperature rise from 25.3 °C and 25.2 °C to 57.3 °C and 57.5 °C (Movie. S2). The results showed that the fiber doped with CQDs enhanced photothermal conversion performance. Fig. 4j showed a graph of the overall temperature change of the fiber under NIR lamp and after NIR lamp removal. In summary, thanks to its photodynamic and photothermal, the resulting BC/CQDs-base yarn exhibited exceptional antibacterial properties, opening new avenues for the development of intelligent thermal management and advanced biomedical textiles.

Among these, A and B represented wet BC/Alg macrofiber and BC/CQDs-based macrofiber, respectively, both of which exhibited a particular level of transparency. S2). The results showed that the fiber doped with CQDs enhanced photothermal conversion performance. Fig. 4j showed a graph of the overall temperature change of the fiber under NIR lamp and after NIR lamp removal. In summary, thanks to its photodynamic and photothermal, the resulting BC/CQDs-base yarn exhibited exceptional antibacterial properties, opening new avenues for the development of intelligent thermal management and advanced biomedical textiles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and biodegradable properties of BC/CQDs-based macrofiber and yarn
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the application of BC/CQDs-based photodynamic antibacterial yarn in the field of wearable textiles, we can construct fabrics with fluorescent feature and antibacterial properties through the weaving process, shining new light for the design and manufacture of advanced biomedical textiles (Fig. 5a). When the prepared BC/CQDs-based yarn with antibacterial properties and mechanically robust was woven into the textile with different patterns, the textile could display an editable fluorescent pattern under UV light, as shown in Fig. 5b–c and Movie. S3. Based on previous research, it has been established that cellulase solution is capable of degrading cellulose. During the degradation process, cellulase initially binds to the surface of the fiber through electrostatic interaction, forming a reaction interface. Subsequently, cellulase is able to cleave the β-1, 4-glucose bond within the fiber chain, either internally or at the ends [63]. As a result, cellulose oligosaccharides produced by cutting are released from the fiber surface, ultimately leading to the degradation of the fiber. Therefore, we further studied the effect of cellulase solution on the degradation performance of biomimetic helical BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. The results showed that the mass of the remaining the resulting BC/CQDs-based macrofiber decreased gradually with the increase of time, and the macrofiber mass lost 65 % when cellulase acted for 96 h, as shown in Fig. 5d. Fig. 5e and Fig. S8 displayed BC/CQDs-based macrofiber photo image of the real-time degradation process every 12 h, and the results showed that the macrofiber was gradually degraded in the cellulase solution. Fig. 5f showed the SEM image of the fiber within 36 h during the degradation process, and it was found that the surface structure and diameter of the sample changed. Among them, the first reason was that the cutting effect of cellulase and macrofiber caused the molecular structure of the macrofiber to change, and some cellulose that had undergone degradation was released from the surface of the macrofiber, causing the fiber to lose part of its mass and volume, so the scale and structure of the sample changed. The second reason was that photographing degraded fibers required taking the sample out of the cellulase solution. At that time, the macrofibers were in a relatively loose state and carried some water. When the sample touched the flat surface, the sample formed a flat state due to the gravity of the water. Therefore, the scale of the SEM images for 12 h and 24 h degradation in Fig. 5f was larger, while the mass loss of the macrofiber was larger after degradation for 36 h, so the diameter of the captured sample decreased.

In order to demonstrate the application of BC/CQDs-based photodynamic antibacterial yarn in the field of wearable textiles, we can construct fabrics with fluorescent feature and antibacterial properties through the weaving process, shining new light for the design and manufacture of advanced biomedical textiles (Fig. 5a). When the prepared BC/CQDs-based yarn with antibacterial properties and mechanically robust was woven into the textile with different patterns, the textile could display an editable fluorescent pattern under UV light, as shown in Fig. 5b–c and Movie. S3. Based on previous research, it has been established that cellulase solution is capable of degrading cellulose. During the degradation process, cellulase initially binds to the surface of the fiber through electrostatic interaction, forming a reaction interface. Subsequently, cellulase is able to cleave the β-1, 4-glucose bond within the fiber chain, either internally or at the ends [63]. As a result, cellulose oligosaccharides produced by cutting are released from the fiber surface, ultimately leading to the degradation of the fiber. Therefore, we further studied the effect of cellulase solution on the degradation performance of biomimetic helical BC/CQDs-based macrofiber. The results showed that the mass of the remaining the resulting BC/CQDs-based macrofiber decreased gradually with the increase of time, and the macrofiber mass lost 65 % when cellulase acted for 96 h, as shown in Fig. 5d. Fig. 5e and Fig. S8 displayed BC/CQDs-based macrofiber photo image of the real-time degradation process every 12 h, and the results showed that the macrofiber was gradually degraded in the cellulase solution. Fig. 5f showed the SEM image of the fiber within 36 h during the degradation process, and it was found that the surface structure and diameter of the sample changed. Among them, the first reason was that the cutting effect of cellulase and macrofiber caused the molecular structure of the macrofiber to change, and some cellulose that had undergone degradation was released from the surface of the macrofiber, causing the fiber to lose part of its mass and volume, so the scale and structure of the sample changed. The second reason was that photographing degraded fibers required taking the sample out of the cellulase solution. At that time, the macrofibers were in a relatively loose state and carried some water. When the sample touched the flat surface, the sample formed a flat state due to the gravity of the water. Therefore, the scale of the SEM images for 12 h and 24 h degradation in Fig. 5f was larger, while the mass loss of the macrofiber was larger after degradation for 36 h, so the diameter of the captured sample decreased.

In this study, CQDs with a photodynamic antibacterial effect were prepared by an economical and feasible one-pot hydrothermal method. Then, with BC as the base, CQDs as the functional material and Alg as the reinforcement, the macrofiber and yarn were prepared by wet-spinning and wet-twisting process. The experimental results showed that the prepared materials had excellent mechanical properties, photothermal properties, and fluorescence properties. The antibacterial experiment showed that the yarn produced ·OH and 1O2 through the photodynamic type I and type II mechanism and demonstrated remarkable antibacterial efficacy, with 99.9999 % (6 log, P < 0.0001) and 96.54 % (1.46 log, P < 0.0004) effectiveness against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. At the same time, under the action of cellulase, the yarn exhibited excellent biodegradability. This paper provides an effective way for the continuous production of biomass multifunctional yarn, which is helpful in promoting the development of green ecological textiles.
